attributes.stem:
A 42-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic for a review of his ongoing heartburn. These symptoms started two years ago and an endoscopy at that time showed low grade oesophagitis only. He was started on esomeprazole 20 mg once daily which initially gave him good control of his symptoms. However, over the last four months his symptoms have worsened and he is getting daily episodes of heartburn. He has no significant medical history and takes no other regular medications. He smokes 20 cigarettes a day, drinks two cans of beer daily and has a BMI of 29 kg/m2. On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Abdominal examination is unremarkable.

Another endoscopy is arranged which shows a 4 cm length segment of Barrett's oesophagus and biopsies of a slightly raised area show low grade dysplasia. Based on these observations, a further endoscopy with more biopsies is performed and the presence of low grade dysplasia confirmed. Nothing more suspicious is found.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Endoscopic mucosal resection of the raised area
Radiofrequency ablation of the Barrett's oesophagus
attributes.explanation:
The current management of low grade dysplasia in a patient with Barrett's oesophagus is evolving. While the risk of malignant change is small (annual risk of developing adenocarcinoma is 0.2-2.0%), this patient needs some plan of active management. This can vary from endoscopic surveillance to some form of endoscopic eradication, with radiofrequency ablation being the preferred technique. After an initial finding of low grade dysplastic changes, this diagnosis needs confirmation with a repeat endoscopy and more extensive biopsies. In this case, it is especially concerning that there is a raised area, as dysplasia is a microscopic diagnosis. Macroscopic changes cannot be ignored and suggest the potential for malignancy. Endoscopic mucosal resection (EMR) of this particular area is warranted to both remove the lesion and allow for careful histological assessment. Further management will depend on the results, but it is likely that endoscopic ablation of the residual Barrett's would be required in due course.

A repeat endoscopy in 12 months would be appropriate in the absence of concerning macroscopic changes. Endoscopic surveillance of Barrett's oesophagus is performed due to the increased risk of developing adenocarcinoma. On an individual basis, this risk is very small, and only becomes real when dysplastic changes are found in the areas of Barrett's oesophagus. Current guidelines recommend:

  • Intestinal metaplasia only: 3 - 5 year surveillance

  • Low grade dysplasia: repeat endoscopy at 6-12 months and if dysplasia still found, offer endoscopic ablation

  • High grade dysplasia: endoscopic ablation

Increasing the dose of esomeprazole may provide better symptomatic relief, but such an approach alone would not address the most important issue, namely the dysplastic changes that have been identified.

Radiofrequency ablation of the Barrett's oesophagus is an effective treatment for flat areas of dysplasia. It involves the application of thermal energy to the oesophageal mucosa, causing tissue destruction and subsequent re-epithelialization with normal squamous epithelium. While it is increasingly used for low grade dysplasia, it is not the most appropriate first step in this case due to the presence of a raised area. Ablation should only be performed after visible lesions have been removed and carefully examined.

Laparoscopic fundoplication could be considered as adjuvant therapy for patients where radiofrequency ablation is being considered. However, it is not the most appropriate next step for managing the dysplastic changes.

In addition to the chosen management, the patient should receive appropriate treatment for his reflux and be counselled on minimising other risk factors for oesophageal cancer, including smoking cessation, reducing alcohol consumption, and weight loss.
learningPoints: Low grade dysplasia in Barrett's oesophagus is now being actively treated with some form of ablation therapy.
Endoscopic mucosal resection (EMR) is indicated for visible lesions in Barrett's oesophagus before considering ablation therapy.
references: Falk GW. Current Management of Low-Grade Dysplasia in Barrett Esophagus Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2017; 13: 221–25.
Katzka DA, Falk GW. Management of Low-Grade Dysplasia in Barrett’s Esophagus: Incremental Progress Continues. Gastroenterology 2017; 152:928–32

Shaheen NJ, Falk GW, Iyer PG, Souza RF, Yadlapati RH, Sauer BG, et al. Diagnosis and Management of Barrett’s Esophagus: An Updated ACG Guideline. Official journal of the American College of Gastroenterology | ACG [Internet]. 2022;117(4).
